they were obtained. It should be noted that full inspiration 
is rarely achieved in guinea pigs due to their rapid respira-
tion rate.
An anatomical study in healthy guinea pigs reported a 
median vertebral heart score (VHS) of 7.5 (interquartile 
range 7.2–8.0) (De Silva et al., 2022). In this study, the 
apicobasal length of the heart (long axis) and the width of 
the heart at its widest point perpendicular to the long axis 
were transposed on to the vertebral column starting from 
the cranial edge of the body of the fourth thoracic vertebra, 
and the VHS was calculated as the sum of the long axis 
and cardiac width expressed as units of vertebral length.
(a)

(a) There is dorsal displacement of the trachea (arrowed) and the 
cardiac silhouette is larger than normal. The stomach is 
distended with gas (G) due to dyspnoea.
Bony spicules
Bony spicules consisting of dense lamellar bone 
with different degrees of calcification have been 
described in the lungs of guinea pigs (Yarto-Jara-
millo, 2011; Ardiaca García et al., 2021). These 
fragments of bone may be misinterpreted as inhaled 
foreign bodies or diagnosed as osseous metaplasia 
from a neoplasm. Although the author has never 
observed this finding on radiography or computed 
tomography (CT), pulmonary osseous metaplasia is 
a common histopathological finding in other mam-
(b) malian species, so it is possible that this pathology 
(a) Ventrodorsal and (b) right lateral thoracic radiographs  also occurs in guinea pigs.

Chapter 16 • Cardiorespiratory disease
Ultrasonography and echocardiography
Thoracic ultrasonography in guinea pigs is performed in 
a similar manner to other species and can be useful for 
the identification and management of respiratory disor-
ders such as pneumothorax, pneumonia, lobar consolida-
tion, atelectasis and pleural effusion. Echocardiography 
is indicated for the diagnosis of structural and functional 
cardiac abnormalities, especially cardiomyopathies, 
abnormal thickness of the pericardium, and valvular and 
other abnormalities, and is also used to assess the flow 
of blood through the heart. Reference echocardiographic 
values have been reported in conscious and anaesthe-
tized healthy guinea pigs (Çetin et al., 2005; De Silva et 
al., 2020) (Figures 16.5 and 16.6). M-mode and B-mode 
ultrasound imaging are used for a complete ultrasono-
graphic examination of the heart. This procedure can be 
performed without sedation, unless fine-needle aspiration 
is to be performed or the patient is distressed or difficult 
to restrain. However, the author recommends the use of 
sedation because the rapid heartbeat and small size of 
guinea pigs necessitates a careful approach to obtain 
high-quality images (Figure 16.7). High-frequency trans-
ducers with small contact surfaces are better for providing 
a good resolution and avoiding interference from the ribs.

BSAVA Manual of Guinea Pigs
Clipping the hair is also recommended. The animal should 
preferably be placed in lateral recumbency. The use of an 
echocardiography table is a matter of clinician preference; 
the author prefers to use one, especially when the heart 
is being evaluated. Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspira-
tion of pleural and pericardial effusions, pulmonary 
abscesses, lobar consolidations and mediastinal masses 
is recommended to obtain samples for cytology, histopa-
thology and culture.
Advanced imaging
Advanced imaging techniques, such as CT (Figures 16.8 
and 16.9) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), may be 
helpful in the diagnosis of cardiorespiratory diseases. Both 
techniques provide images without the summation of 
adjacent anatomical structures. Because of the short time 
required for scanning and the high resolution of the images 
obtained, CT is the preferred modality. CT images are 
stored and analysed by a computer that constructs new 
(a)
images with the overlying structures removed. The normal 
thoracic structure of the rabbit has been described with 
CT, and measurements of the trachea, heart, thoracic 
caudal vena cava and aorta, right and left principal bronchi, 
right and left caudal lobar bronchi and the accompanying 
branches of the right and left pulmonary artery and vein, 
right and left lung volume and lung density made on CT 
images have been published (Müllhaupt et al., 2017). A 
similar study to define the exact topographical features of 
thoracic structures has been performed in guinea pigs; 
the heart was situated at the level of the 4th and 9th ribs 
and close to the midline of the thorax due to the equal size 
of the lungs. This study also included a morphometric 
study of the ventral cavity volume (thoracic cavity + 
abdominal cavity) and found that the thoracic cavity makes 
up 20.05% of the ventral cavity volume and the abdominal 
cavity makes up the remaining 79.95%, and 62.28% of the 
thoracic cavity volume is related to the lungs and 14.04% 
to the heart (Zehtabvar et al., 2023).
Rhinitis, sinusitis, upper respiratory tract diseases and 
(b)
lung lesions may be better assessed with CT than with 
radiography (Ardiaca García et al., 2021). Exudate-filled  Computed tomographic images of the skull of a healthy

addition, cases of chronic otitis or upper respiratory tract 
disease may involve the central nervous system. In these 
cases, MRI may be useful as it provides better results than 
CT when evaluating soft tissues. A high-resolution MRI 
device should be used to obtain good images in small 
species such as guinea pigs. Information about MRI of the 
guinea pig thorax is sparse. In one study, MRI was used to 
observe lesions in the lungs of guinea pigs infected with 
Mycobacterium tuberculosis by low-dose aerosol expo-
sure (Kraft et al., 2004). A 1.5 T Signa HiSpeed LX 9.0 (GE 
Medical Systems) machine was used. Small hyperintense 
nodules could be seen in the lung parenchyma in 
T1-weighted images. Severe lymphadenopathy of the 
mediastinal lymph nodes was observed. On the basis of 
this report, MRI is a useful tool for assessing the develop-
ment of lung pathology.
Electrocardiography
Electrocardiography can be performed in conscious, 
sedated or anaesthetized guinea pigs. The patient can be 
positioned in ventral, dorsal or right lateral recumbency 
for the examination. Ventral recumbency (Figure 16.10a)  A computed tomographic image of the thorax of a healthy 
16.9
is preferred in conscious patients as this position induces  guinea pig, obtained using a lung window. h = heart.

Thoracocentesis
Many cardiac diseases are associated with pleural and/or 
pericardial effusion and, in some cases, abdominal effu-
sion. Ultrasound-guided thoracocentesis and pericardio-
centesis are routinely performed to obtain samples of fluid 
for cytology, histopathology and microbiological culture 
(Figure 16.12). Fluid samples can also be analysed for 
specific gravity, protein concentration, cell count and 
macroscopic appearance. See Chapter 5 for a description 
of the technique for thoracocentesis.

Pericarditis
Pericarditis is the result of inflammation of the pericar-
dium. It is caused by infection, often with Streptococcus 
pneumoniae. Fibrinopurulent pleuritis, peritonitis and 
epicarditis have also been associated with S. pneumoniae 
infection (Parker et al., 1977). This condition is similar to 
cardiac tamponade, except that the major contributor to 
the cardiac compression is the non-distensible, thickened, 
fibrotic pericardium rather than the accumulation of peri-
cardial fluid. Dias et al. (2016) described a case of acute 
diffuse fibrinous necrotizing pericarditis with intralesional 
bacteria (S. pneumoniae) in a 1.5-month-old entire sow 
with pleuropneumonia. This condition is rarely reported 
in guinea pigs.
Myocarditis
Myocarditis is the result of inflammation of the myocardium. 
It can be caused by infection. One report described an 
outbreak of a Streptococcus infection in a population of 
guinea pigs following introduction of new animals to the

Cytology of a sample of pericardial fluid was compatible 
with a haemorrhagic effusion, but the aetiology could not 
be confirmed. After pericardiocentesis, a significant 
improvement in the clinical signs and left ventricular sys-
tolic function was observed; 24 hours later, the guinea pig 
was clinically normal. At re-examination 6 months later 
there was no recurrence of the pericardial effusion; the 
guinea pig lived for 25 months after the initial examination 
and died of an undetermined cause.
A malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour affecting 
the dorsal subcutis and periphery of the spine was identi-
fied in a 3-month-old sow presented with pleural and 
haemorrhagic pericardial effusions (Ueda et al., 2019). 
No metastases were observed in the heart on post-
mortem examination, and pleural and pericardial effusions 
are common findings in dogs and cats with this type of 
tumour.
(a) Toxicosis
The ingestion of oleander (Nerium oleander) plants can 
cause cardiac dysrhythmias (among other signs) in the 
guinea pig, which may be treated successfully with appro-
priate supportive care (Heatley, 2009). The leaves, fruits, 
bark and seeds of the avocado are toxic to guinea pigs 
and other exotic species when ingested. The diagnosis is 
suspected on the basis of a history of ingestion. Clinical 
signs include respiratory distress, anasarca (severe gen-
eralized oedema), hydropericardium and sometimes death. 
The patient should be treated as soon as possible to try 
to prevent toxin absorption; treatment with gastric lavage, 
activated charcoal, bulking diets and, in critical cases, 
oxygen therapy, diuretics and supportive care has been 
reported (Yarto-Jaramillo, 2011). See Appendix 3 for further 
information about toxic plants and substances.

Influenza
Guinea pigs are susceptible to influenza viruses. 
Influenza infection is largely concentrated in the 
upper respiratory tract and results in few clinical 
signs. In a laboratory setting, guinea pigs can be 
infected with influenza viruses and efficiently 
transmit the viruses to naive hosts without show-
ing any overt clinical signs of disease (Lowen et al., 
2006). Influenza viruses can be transmitted from 
infected to non-infected guinea pigs housed either 
in the same cage or in adjacent cages. Animals 
suspected of having influenza should be isolated. 
It is not known whether guinea pigs can be natu-
rally infected with influenza viruses.
Congenital and hereditary conditions
Congenital and hereditary conditions of the upper res-
piratory tract are very uncommon. Cleft palate has been 
reported in a 3-day-old guinea pig  (Ardiaca García et al., 
2021).
Neoplasia

Chapter 16 • Cardiorespiratory disease
Lower respiratory tract
Bacterial pneumonia 
Bacterial pneumonia is commonly seen in guinea pigs. 
Bordetella, Streptococcus, Klebsiella, Pasteurella, Yers-
inia, Salmonella, Pseudomonas and Diplococcus spp. 
have all been isolated from guinea pigs with pneumonia 
(Rigby, 1976; Ardiaca García et al., 2021). In addition, 
guinea pigs can naturally harbour Bordetella bronchisep-
tica and Streptococcus pneumoniae and may be asymp-
tomatic carriers of these bacteria (Roberts-Steel et al., 
2019). Overt disease arises when an animal is under 
(a)
stress as a result of inappropriate husbandry (e.g. poor 
hygiene and ventilation), nutritional disorders (particu-
larly hypovitaminosis C), or the introduction of new 
cagemates or co-housing with rabbits, which are nor-
mally colonized with B. bronchiseptica and Pasteurella 
multocida. Both B. bronchiseptica and S. pneumoniae 
can cause purulent bronchopneumonia and fibrinosup-
purative pleuritis.
Clinical signs: Common clinical signs include nasal and 
ocular discharge, dyspnoea, tachypnoea, abdominal 
respiratory effort, apathy and anorexia. Coughing may 
rarely be seen. Concurrent middle ear infections are 
common and therefore radiographs of the head as well 
as the thorax are always recommended in cases of res-
piratory disease in guinea pigs (Figure 16.19). When the 
inner ear is affected, animals present with peripheral 
vestibular syndrome, head tilt, strabismus and circling. 
Subclinical pneumonia should be considered in cases of 
otitis media and/or otitis interna in guinea pigs (see 
Chapter 21).
Diagnosis: Diagnosis of bacterial pneumonia is based 
on clinical signs, blood analyses, cytological examination 
of samples of any nasal or ocular discharges, diagnostic 
imaging (Figures 16.20–16.22) and microbiological cul-
ture. Gram staining and cytology can help in the identi-
fication of the causative bacterium: B. bronchiseptica is 
a Gram-negative rod and S. pneumoniae is a Gram-
positive coccus.
Treatment: The treatment for bacterial pneumonia com-
prises antibiotic therapy for 7–31 days, depending on the 
changes in the patient’s condition, with supportive treat-
ment, supplemental oxygen, fluid therapy, analgesia and 
assisted feeding if necessary. Systemic antibiotics should 
be administered until the bacterial infection is eliminated 
(b)
from the body, which may be some days after the clinical 
signs have resolved. Thoracic radiographs should be  16.19 (a) Right lateral thoracic and abdominal, and (b) ventro-

Mycoplasmal pneumonia
Experimental infection with Mycoplasma pneumo-
niae caused pulmonary lesions in young guinea pigs 
(Brunner et al., 1973). All four animals presented with 
sniffing, sneezing, nasal discharge and/or dysp-
noea. Mycoplasmal interstitial pneumonia was 
diagnosed based on pathological, morphological 
and histopathological examinations. The aetiology 
was confirmed by electron microscopy. In addition, 
Mycoplasma caviae was isolated from the naso-
pharynx and vagina in 10% of 223 guinea pigs (Hill, 
1971); this isolate seemed to be non-pathogenic to 
rodents. Pneumonia as a result of Mycoplasma spp. 
infection should be considered in relevant cases.
(d)
